data.title: John Hancock Global Shareholder Yield Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary 1 Highlights

  • The MSCI World Index, the fund's benchmark, produced a strong return in the third quarter.
  • The fund, while positive in absolute terms, didn't keep pace with the benchmark.
  • Dividend-paying stocks were generally out of favor at a time of heightened investor risk appetites.

Market review and outlook

Global equities rallied in the quarter, as a favorable combination of steady economic growth, falling interest rates across the developed markets, and waning concerns about tariffs proved highly supportive for equities. Dividend stocks generally lagged the broader indexes, however. Investors remained focused on more speculative areas of the market, as well as high-growth themes related to artificial intelligence.

The U.S. economy remained largely resilient, but signs of some weakness in the labor market have raised questions about the economic outlook. The geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrop has been challenging outside of the United States, with ongoing geopolitical tensions, broad deglobalization, and a realignment of global supply chains from tariffs and onshoring/reshoring threatening to suppress long-term growth. Given the uncertain macro environment and stretched valuations in the stock market, we believe the potential for elevated volatility is high. While we're cautiously optimistic on the path forward for the economy and equities from a fundamental standpoint, we think these factors indicate the need for vigilance and an emphasis on risk management.

We believe the fund is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resilience if growth begins to falter. We've sought to build a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and long-standing track records of sound capital allocation practices. We maintain conviction that these types of companies should have the ability to adapt and endure through potential disruptions. We believe diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against uncertainty and volatility.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's relative performance was pressured by an underweight position in the IT sector, particularly a zero weighting in Nvidia, Inc. (NVDA) The fund doesn't hold the stock because it doesn't offer an attractive dividend, but the stock continued to outperform the broader market. An underweight in Apple (AAPL), which recovered from its weakness in the first half of the year thanks to strong orders for the new iPhone, further detracted. On the other hand, a number of tech stocks—including Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. (TSM), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co., and Dell Technologies, Inc. (DELL)—were among the fund's top individual contributors for the quarter. Outside of technology, notable detractors included overweight positions in Philip Morris International, Inc. (PM), LyondellBasell Industries NV (LYB), and Texas Instruments, Inc.

On the positive side, the fund outperformed in healthcare thanks to strength in several pharmaceutical stocks. A position in AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), whose shares rose following a patent extension for a key drug, was the top contributor. Holdings in the Japanese company NHK Spring Co., Ltd. also contributed, as did the U.S. engine producer Cummins, Inc. (CMI)

Portfolio changes

We initiated new positions in Infrastruttura Wireless Italiane S.p.A. and Google parent Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), and we sold the fund's holdings in Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), Dow, Inc. (DOW), and Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB)

data.title: American Century Small Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

U.S. stocks gained ground. Equities rose, with several reaching record highs. The Federal Reserve, reacting to job market weakness, reduced its benchmark interest rate for the first time this year. Most policymakers expected two more rate cuts by the end of the year.

Economic growth continues. The economy continued to expand, which helped stocks overcome concerns about inflation, a U.S. government shutdown and ongoing tariff uncertainty. The U.S. government revised its final reading of second-quarter gross domestic product (a measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy) from 3.3% to 3.8%, marking a sharp rebound from the first quarter.

Energy detracted. Stock selection in this sector hampered relative performance. Energy-related stocks dramatically underperformed the benchmark as increased production by OPEC members helped push gas and oil prices lower amid tempered demand.

Financials underperformed. This sector lagged due to overweight positioning in the financial services and insurance industries relative to the benchmark. Stock selection also hindered performance. In financial services, Euronet Worldwide and Evertec were notable detractors from returns. In insurance, AXIS Capital Holdings and Slide Insurance Holdings (SLDE) also detracted.

Utilities contributed. Utilities stocks narrowly underperformed the benchmark. As a result, our lack of exposure to the sector contributed to performance.

Key Contributors

The Brink's Co. (BCO) Shares of this cash-in-transit/armored truck service provider performed well during the quarter as the company reported strong earnings, benefiting from its strategic shift toward premium solutions.

Axcelis Technologies (ACLS). After an extended period of underperformance, we think the stock of this semiconductor capital equipment company has performed well. Axcelis has been generating better-than-expected profits amid stabilizing demand. We think the stock has also likely benefited from improved semiconductor sentiment.

Visteon (VC). This supplier of displays to automakers fell out of favor as investors feared that tariffs could limit automobile production. But the company has won new business with Asian original equipment manufacturers (outside of China).

Key Detractors

Americold Realty Trust (COLD). This real estate investment trust (REIT), which specializes in cold storage in the food supply chain, has been hurt by high inflation and interest rates. Declining food volumes due to the popularity of GLP-1 drugs have also hurt earnings.

Axis Capital Holdings. Shares of this property and casualty insurer and reinsurer underperformed as investors rotated out of insurance stocks, favoring what they believed were higher-risk sectors and industries with greater growth potential.

Euronet Worldwide. This financial services provider detracted. Despite Euronet's strong financial characteristics, we think the stock has been hindered by the Trump administration's immigration policies, which could hurt the money transfer business, and by the rise of stablecoins.

Notable Trades

Bath & Body Works (BBWI). We initiated a position in this retailer of fragrance and body care products for what we believed were its high margins and potential for rapid growth. We think the company's low valuation should be temporary and that strong free cash flows can help mitigate risk as we wait for strategic initiatives to take hold.

Ashland Global Holdings (ASH). We opened a position in this global specialty chemical company, which has shifted its focus to serving the consumer and pharmaceutical end markets. Despite what we believe is a high-quality portfolio, the stock is trading at a steep discount to historical valuations.

GMS (GMS). This specialty building products distributor was acquired by The Home Depot (HD) following a bidding war with QXO (QXO), another building products distributor. We exited the position on the deal.

ChampionX (CHX). This oil field services company was acquired by SLB (formerly Schlumberger (SLB)). The transaction closed out our position.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio seeks to invest in small-cap companies where we believe the valuation does not reflect the quality and normal earnings power of the company. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

Opportunities in financials. We have been significantly overweight in this sector relative to the benchmark, especially in banks, financial services companies (e.g., payment processors, holding companies and metals brokers) and insurers. We also have no exposure to mortgage REITs and consumer finance. Our research has indicated an impending positive inflection in net interest income and overstated fears by the market for an extended negative lending cycle. We remain positively predisposed to what we believe are higher-quality banks, with many of our holdings still trading meaningfully below historical valuations.

Overweight consumer discretionary. We have held a large overweight in this sector relative to the benchmark. We believe our holdings have been attractively valued and have provided us with exposure to unique and more durable areas of the consumer market (e.g., higher-end consumers, recurring revenue stories, etc.). Our overweight in the sector is broadly dispersed across what we believe are higher-quality specialty retail companies, higher-end accessories companies, leisure product manufacturers, consumer services companies, leisure companies and household durables providers.

Less weight in industrials. We reduced our allocation in this sector, but the portfolio is still overweight relative to the benchmark. We have continued to find what we believe are higher-quality industrials (e.g., machinery, distribution, commercial services), many of which have been able to exert strong pricing power during the recent inflationary environment. Our research has indicated that this pricing power, coupled with strong core unit demand and prudent cost control efforts, has enabled many of our industrials to generate significant excess cash flow and has allowed accelerated efforts to return cash to shareholders via increased dividends, share buybacks and debt reduction.

No exposure to utilities. We have no exposure to utilities stocks. After underperforming the benchmark in 2023 and again in 2024, the sector snapped back in the first quarter of 2025, providing what we believe was defensive posturing for investors in the face of a volatile market. The sector lagged once again in the second and third quarters of 2025 as investor appetite for risk returned. We continue to look for opportunities to gain exposure in this sector, though at this point we believe there have been better risk/reward opportunities in more cyclical corners of the market.

Significantly underweight health care. We believe valuations in the health care sector have not presented attractive risk/reward opportunities. A significant portion of our underweight in this sector relative to the benchmark has been driven by our lack of exposure to the biotechnology, pharmaceuticals and life sciences tools and services industries. We will likely remain materially underweight in the health care sector as we believe these industries, which now account for a significant portion of the benchmark, do not fit our process regarding quality and financial strength.

data.title: Tracking Leon Cooperman's Omega Advisors Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Leon Cooperman’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on his regulatory 13F form filed on 11/14/2025. The 13F portfolio value increased from ~$2.85B to ~$3.20B this quarter. Recent 13F reports have shown a total of around 45 individual stock positions in the portfolio, although many of them are minutely small. The largest five stakes are Mr. Cooper Group, Vertiv Holdings, Energy Transfer, MP Materials, and Mirion Technologies. They add up to ~48% of the entire portfolio. Please visit our Tracking Leon Cooperman’s Omega Advisors Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the activity last quarter.

New Stakes:

Amrize Limited (AMRZ): AMRZ is 1.33% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between ~$44 and ~$56. The stock currently trades at $50.20.

Stake Disposals:

Fiserv Inc. (FISV): FISV was 2.36% of the portfolio position. The stake goes back to a large position in First Data Corporation purchased in Q4 2015. Fiserv acquired First Data in an all-stock deal (0.303 of Fiserv for each First Data share) that closed in July 2019. At the time, the 2M share stake in Fiserv was the largest position in the portfolio at ~13%. There was a ~50% selling during Q1 2020 at prices between ~$82 and ~$122. Recent activity follows. Q4 2024 saw a ~20% reduction in prices between ~$181 and ~$223. The disposal this quarter was at prices between ~$127 and ~$176. The stock is now at $60.80.

Stake Increases:

Mirion Technologies (MIR): MIR is 6.09% of the portfolio position purchased during Q4 2021 at prices between $9.84 and $11.76. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows. There was a ~13% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$11 and ~$19. This quarter saw an ~18% stake increase at prices between ~$18 and ~$25. The stock currently trades at $25.85.

Note: Mirion Technologies came to market through a SPAC merger with GS Acquisition II that closed in October 2021.

Fidelis Insurance (FIHL): FIHL is a 3.78% of the portfolio stake established during 2023 in the low-teens price range. The three quarters through Q4 2024 saw the position more than double at prices between ~$15 and ~$21. There was a ~28% further increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$14 and ~$18. That was followed by a ~20% increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$14.50 and ~$18. This quarter saw a ~15% further increase at prices between ~$15 and ~$18. The stock is now at $19.04.

Note: They have a ~6.4% ownership stake in the business.

Elevance Health (ELV): The 3.44% of the portfolio ELV position was purchased during 2023 at prices between ~$429 and ~$495. H2 2024 saw the stake more than double at prices between ~$366 and ~$562. There was a ~20% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$274 and ~$401. The stock currently trades at ~$339.

Cigna Corp. (CI): The 2.92% CI stake was first purchased during the three quarters through Q1 2019 at prices between ~$161 and ~$223. There was a ~22% reduction during Q3 2020 at prices between ~$162 and ~$191. Recent activity follows. Q3 2024 saw ~11% selling at prices between ~$319 and ~$367. That was followed by another similar reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$296 and ~$350. This quarter saw a ~53% stake increase at prices between ~$257 and ~$339. The stock is now at ~$278.

Sunoco Inc. (SUN): The SUN position was established during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$51 and ~$60. The last quarter saw the position built at prices between ~$48 and ~$60. The stock currently trades at $55.95 and the stake is at 2.53% of the portfolio. This quarter saw a ~10% increase.

Atlas Energy (AESI): AESI is 1.90% of the portfolio position established during the last quarter at prices between ~$12 and ~$19. The stock currently trades below that range at $8.34. There was a minor ~4% increase this quarter.

GE Healthcare (GEHC): The 1.41% GEHC stake was purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$58 and ~$81. This quarter saw a ~50% stake increase at prices between ~$69 and ~$80. The stock now goes for $80.63.

Enterprise Product Partners (EPD), Finance of America (FOA), Gannett Company (GCI), Manchester United (MANU), and SunOpta Inc. (STKL): These stakes were increased this quarter.

Note: They have significant ownership stakes in Finance of America, Manchester United, and SunOpta.

Stake Decreases:

MP Materials (MP): The 6.28% MP stake was built in H2 2020 at prices between ~$10 and ~$35.50. The position has wavered. Recent activity follows. Q2 2024 saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$12 and ~$19. There was a ~10% trimming during the last quarter at prices between ~$18.50 and ~$39. The stock currently trades at $60.15. This quarter saw a ~7% trimming.

Note: Fortress Value Acquisition merged with MP Materials in a ~$1B EV SPAC deal that closed in November 2020.

Arbor Realty Trust (ABR): ABR is 0.65% of the portfolio position built during Q1 2019 at prices between ~$10.50 and ~$13. There was a ~17% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$11.50 and ~$14.50. That was followed by a ~10% trimming during the last quarter at prices between ~$8.75 and ~$12. The stock is now at $9.03. This quarter saw a ~6% trimming.

OneMain Holdings (OMF): The 1.48% OMF stake saw a ~25% reduction this quarter.

Kept Steady:

Mr. Cooper Group (COOP): Mr. Cooper was the largest 13F position at ~19% of the portfolio. The original small stake was doubled in Q4 2018 in the mid-teens price range following its 12-for-1 reverse stock split and name change transaction. That original stake was since sold down, but a larger stake was built during Q4 2019 & Q1 2020 at prices between ~$6 and ~$14.50. There was a ~15% trimming during Q1 2021 at prices between ~$27 and ~$37. Rocket Companies (RKT) acquired Mr. Cooper in a cash-and-stock deal (11 Rocket shares for each Mr. Cooper, plus a $2.00 per share dividend) that closed last month.

Vertiv Holdings (VRT): VRT is ~10% of the portfolio position built during H2 2020 at prices between ~$17 and ~$20. The next four quarters saw a ~55% stake increase at prices between ~$18 and ~$28. Recent activity follows. Q1 2024 saw a ~11% trimming at prices between ~$46 and ~$82. The stock currently trades at ~$172.

Energy Transfer Equity (ET): ET is a large (top three) ~7% of the portfolio stake established in the Q2 2017 to Q1 2018 timeframe at prices between ~$16 and ~$19. 2019 saw a ~125% stake increase at prices between ~$11.50 and ~$15.75. Q4 2020 also saw a ~30% increase in prices between ~$5 and ~$7. Recent activity follows. The last several quarters saw incremental buying. The stock currently trades at $16.43.

Apollo Global (APO): A large position in Athene was first purchased in Q3 2020 at prices between ~$30 and ~$37. There was a ~60% stake increase in Q1 2021 at prices between ~$41 and ~$52. Apollo Global (APO) acquired Athene in an all-stock (1.140 shares of Apollo for each Athene stock held) deal that closed in January 2022. Recent activity follows. The two quarters through Q3 2024 saw ~17% selling at prices between ~$100 and ~$130. The stock currently trades at ~$130, and the stake is at 5.56% of the portfolio.

Regal Rexnord Corp. (RRX): RRX is 3.29% of the portfolio position purchased during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$115 and ~$151. There was a ~25% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$153 and ~$182. Q1 2025 saw a ~36% stake increase at prices between ~$111 and ~$168. The stock is now at ~$146.

Lithia Motors (LAD): LAD is 3.13% of the portfolio position established during Q2 2021 at prices between ~$313 and ~$403. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows. H2 2022 saw a huge buildup at prices between ~$185 and ~$281. There was a ~25% reduction during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$303 and ~$394. The stock is now at ~$319.

WillScot Holdings (WSC): The 2.66% of the portfolio stake in WSC was built over the four quarters through Q4 2021 at prices between ~$17 and ~$32. There was a ~20% stake increase during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$37 and ~$45. Q4 2024 also saw a ~10% stake increase at prices between ~$33 and ~$40. The stock is now at $20.18.

KBR Inc. (KBR): KBR is 2.58% of the portfolio position purchased during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$55 and ~$72. There was a ~15% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$47 and ~$61. The stock currently trades at $40.77.

Ashland Inc. (ASH): ASH is a long-term stake that was first purchased during H2 2014 at prices between ~$48 and ~$59. Q2 & Q3 2015 saw a stake doubling at prices between ~$50 and ~$63. The position has wavered. Recent activity follows. H2 2024 saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$71 and ~$97. That was followed by a ~17% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$55 and ~$73. The stock is now at $52.54, and the stake is at 2.54% of the portfolio.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the Valvoline (VVV) spin-off in 2017.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): GOOGL is now a very small position at 0.57% of the portfolio. It was first purchased in Q2 2015 at prices between ~$26 and ~$28. The stake was sold in H1 2020 at prices between ~$54 and ~$76 but was rebuilt next quarter at prices between ~$72 and ~$82. The position was sold down by ~85% during the last quarter at prices between ~$143 and ~$183. The stock is now at ~$320.

Motorola Solutions (MSI) and Sea Limited (SE): These stakes were kept steady this quarter.

data.title: Health Care's Epic Rally
data.description:
As the Tech/AI trade has hit a speed bump this month, the Health Care sector has been shot out of a cannon. As of Wednesday's close, the S&P 500 Health Care sector was 10% above its 50-day moving average and 2.5 standard deviations above its 50 DMA. Check out the one-year trading range chart of the sector below:

Health Care's move in the last month has left a good chunk of the stocks in the sector at new 52-week highs. Net new 52-week highs for the sector have exploded to the highest level of the last year:

So far this month, the Health Care sector is outperforming the broad S&P 500 by more than ten percentage points. As shown below, that hasn't happened since November 2000!

While Health Care is catching a bid these days, it's still deep in the hole based on its actions over the last couple of years. Just over two years ago, Health Care's weighting in the S&P 500 was at 16%. By this September, its weighting had nearly been cut in half, down to just 8.65%, its lowest weighting since August 1994.

Health Care's 18% gain in the last two months has only pushed its weight in the S&P back up to 10% as of Thursday, so there's plenty of runway left for the sector if the good vibes continue.

Below is a look at the most extreme overbought stocks in the sector right now. Even names like Bristol Myers (BMY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) have joined in on the rally.

Of course, after moves like this, most traders would tell you to wait for some downside mean reversion before jumping in on the long side.

data.title: S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index: Home Prices Increase For Second Straight Month
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Home prices increased in September as the benchmark national index rose for a second straight month. The seasonally adjusted home prices for the national index saw a 0.2% increase month-over-month and a 1.3% increase year-over-year.

This marks the eighth straight month annual gains have slowed and is the smallest annual increase since July 2023. After adjusting for inflation, the monthly change fell to 0.1%, marking the first monthly increase in real home prices in eight months, and the annual change fell to -2.4%.

The S&P Case-Shiller benchmark 20-City composite aims to measure the value of residential real estate in the following 20 major U.S. cities: Atlanta, Boston, Charlotte, Chicago, Cleveland, Dallas, Denver, Detroit, Las Vegas, Los Angeles, Miami, Minneapolis, New York, Phoenix, Portland, San Diego, San Francisco, Seattle, Tampa, and Washington, D.C.

The benchmark 20-city index increased for a second straight month in September. The seasonally adjusted home prices for the 20-city index saw a 0.1% increase month-over-month and a 1.4% increase year-over-year.

This marks the eighth straight month annual gains have slowed and is the smallest annual increase since July 2023. After adjusting for inflation, the monthly change was reduced to 0.0%, and the annual change fell to -2.3%.

The S&P Case-Shiller benchmark 10-City composite, a subset of the 20-city index, aims to measure the change in value of residential real estate in the following 10 major U.S. cities: Boston, Chicago, Denver, Las Vegas, Los Angeles, Miami, New York, San Diego, San Francisco, and Washington, D.C.

The benchmark 10-city index was up for a second straight month in September. The seasonally adjusted home prices for the 10-city index saw a 0.1% increase month-over-month and a 2.0% increase year-over-year.

This marks the eighth straight month annual gains have slowed and is the smallest annual increase since July 2023. After adjusting for inflation, the monthly change was reduced to 0.0%, and the annual change fell to -1.7%.

Here is the analysis from Tuesday's Standard & Poor's press release:

ANALYSIS

“The housing market's deceleration accelerated in September, with the National Composite posting just a 1.3% annual gain—the weakest performance since mid-2023,” said Nicholas Godec, CFA, CAIA, CIPM, Head of Fixed Income Tradables & Commodities at S&P Dow Jones Indices. “This marks a continued slide from August’s 1.4% increase and represents a stark contrast to the double-digit gains that characterized the early post-pandemic era. National home prices continued trailing inflation, with September’s CPI running 1.7 percentage points ahead of housing appreciation. This marks the widest gap between inflation and home-price growth since the two measures diverged in June, with the spread continuing to widen each month.

“Regional performance reveals a tale of two markets. Chicago continues to lead with a 5.5% annual gain, followed by New York at 5.2% and Boston at 4.1%. These Northeastern and Midwestern metros have sustained momentum even as broader market conditions soften. At the opposite extreme, Tampa posted a 4.1% annual decline—the sharpest drop among tracked metros and its 11th consecutive month of negative annual returns. Phoenix (-2.0%), Dallas (-1.3%), and Miami (-1.3%) likewise remained in negative territory, highlighting particular weakness in Sun Belt markets that experienced the most dramatic pandemic-era price surges.

“The geographic rotation is striking. Markets that were pandemic darlings—particularly in Florida, Arizona, and Texas—are now experiencing outright price declines. Meanwhile, traditionally stable metros in the Northeast and Midwest continue to post solid gains, suggesting a reversion to pre-pandemic patterns where job markets and urban fundamentals drive appreciation rather than migration trends and remote-work dynamics.

“September’s monthly performance was uniformly weak. All 20 tracked metros posted month-over-month declines before seasonal adjustment, with Tampa (-1.0%), San Diego (-0.9%), and Seattle (-0.9%) leading the downturn. Even after seasonal adjustment, the National Index managed only a 0.2% gain. This broad-based monthly weakness suggests that elevated mortgage rates—which remained near 6.3% in late September—are finally overwhelming the market’s supply constraints.

“A deeper look at momentum shows clear weakening. Over the past six months, national home prices have risen just 0.4%, a gain that is only marginal in nominal terms and negative in real inflation-adjusted terms. The deceleration from early-2025 strength has been broad-based, with only seven of the 20 metros—Chicago, Cleveland, Minneapolis, Boston, New York, Charlotte, and Atlanta—posting positive price appreciation over the trailing six-month period. Most Sun Belt and Western markets experienced outright declines, underscoring how affordability pressures and higher mortgage rates have eroded momentum across much of the country.

“For context, this represents the weakest annual price growth since early 2023, when the market was absorbing the initial shock of the Federal Reserve’s aggressive rate-hiking cycle,” Godec concluded. “Yet unlike that period, which saw a quick rebound, current conditions suggest more persistent headwinds. With mortgage rates stubbornly elevated and affordability at multi-decade lows, the market appears to be settling into a new equilibrium of minimal price growth—or, in some regions, outright decline.”

The chart below, which uses seasonally adjusted data, is an overlay of the Case-Shiller 10- and 20-City Composite Indexes along with the national index since 1987, the first year that the 10-city composite was tracked. The 20-city, which is probably the most closely watched of the three, dates from 2000. All three indexes reached their all-time nominal highs in February 2025.

This next chart shows the real inflation-adjusted indexes using the CPI owner's equivalent rent of residences. The national index reached its real all-time high in May 2022, while the 10-city and 20-city composites reached theirs during the housing bubble in March 2006.

The next chart shows the year-over-year Case-Shiller series, again using the seasonally adjusted data.

Here is the same chart as above, but it has been adjusted for inflation using the CPI owner's equivalent rent of residences.

Here is a look at the seasonally and inflation-adjusted Case-Shiller price index from 1987, the first year that monthly data is available (note here we are using the regular CPI, which is indexed to 1982, not 2000).

To get an even better idea of the trend in housing prices over long time periods, we compare the change in the seasonally adjusted Case-Shiller Home Price Index and the CPI. Since 1987, home prices have grown 412%, the consumer price index is up 191%, and real home prices are up only 76%.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include the iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

data.title: Energizer: Almost A Call Option Here
data.description:
A little over a year ago, I believed that Energizer (ENR) was stabilizing but far from energized. Acquisitions pursued in recent years failed to deliver on the promised benefits, causing a real debt overhang in the meantime.

Even as adjusted earnings multiples looked low, the company continued to operate from a very challenged position that included negative organic growth and high leverage. While stabilization revealed some green shoots, much more work was to be done.

While 2025 was a year of stagnation, the company has actually seen debt increase amidst continued dividends, small M&A efforts, and even share buybacks. With further pressure seen on EBITDA metrics in 2026, leverage ratios in the near term will only increase, making it hard to build conviction.

Responsibly Create Household Products

Energizer aims to responsibly create household products to make lives better. The company is a nearly $3 billion business that relies largely on batteries & lights, which make up nearly 80% of sales, complemented by auto care activities. Two-thirds of these activities are generated in North America, complemented by international operations.

The business has been quite acquisitive after major M&A efforts with Spectrum after it acquired the battery and portable lighting business from that company in a $2 billion deal in 2018. With this deal, it added well-known battery names like Varta and Rayovac to its lineup of brands like Energizer, Eveready, and many more. This transaction was followed by another $1.25 billion deal to acquire the auto care business of Spectrum.

Following these M&A efforts, revenues have nearly doubled to $3 billion over the past decade, as operating margins have been quite stable in the high teens. M&A came at the expense of an increasing debt load, but also about 15% dilution incurred in the shareholder base over this period of time, all creating quite a debt overhang.

A Base

Given the lack of structural value created, I was not surprised to see shares trade at just $28 in the fall of 2024, compared to numbers in the $60s a decade earlier.

Late in 2024, the company guided for full-year earnings around $3.25 per share, earnings that were badly needed as net debt of $3.1 billion was very substantial, equal to 5.0 times anticipated EBITDA. The company halted buybacks and continued to deliver on cost-saving programs in order to address these issues. While lower earnings multiples around 10 times looked interesting, and a 4% dividend yield was paid, I failed to have conviction as stabilization in the business still felt a bit shaky.

Since the fall of last year, shares of Energizer have gradually fallen to a low of $20 over the summer, temporarily peaking at $30, as shares have fallen to lows of $17 per share in recent weeks.

Continued Challenges

At the start of the year, the company reported a more than 2% fall in 2024 sales to $2.89 billion. While adjusted earnings rose by twenty-three cents to $3.32 per share, GAAP earnings only came in at $0.52 per share. While I am happy to adjust for amortization charges, litigation and restructuring costs are very real.

Net debt just came in below $3 billion, at $2.99 billion, to be precise, as EBITDA topped $600 million. The company guided for a modest increase in 2025 EBITDA to $625-$645 million, with adjusted earnings seen up to $3.45-$3.65 per share.

After a solid first quarter, the company trimmed the full-year guidance in a minor fashion in the second quarter, only to increase the full-year earnings guidance in a small manner in the third quarter. By now, organic sales were seen as between flat and plus 2%, with reported sales growth a point higher. At this point in time, the company saw EBITDA between $630 and $640 million, with earnings seen between $3.55 and $3.65 per share.

So far the relatively good news is that this stabilization has not prevented net debt from rising to $3.19 billion, up two hundred million since the start of the year. This came amidst poor cash flow conversion, but also as the company paid $66 million in dividends in the first nine months of the year, bought back $63 million worth of stock, as well as announced a bolt-on deal for AS NV. All this makes the leverage ratios dead flat at 5.0 times EBITDA.

And Now?

Frankly, Energizer is quite a stable business, one that is posting solid margins on that stable revenue base, yet the simple issue is that of debt. Amidst dividends payable, shares being bought back, and M&A efforts pursued, earnings being highly adjusted, net debt has increased a bit.

This makes it so that leverage is no longer coming down, and this is what likely spooks the market, certainly as earnings are quite adjusted. The promise of deleveraging is not coming through, as I simply feel that investors are too concerned about this leverage situation in between the struggling positioned business, as well as adjusted earnings and dividend commitment.

Recently, the company announced softer fourth-quarter results, with organic growth reported at just 0.7%. The softer quarter made that full-year earnings of $3.52 per share and EBITDA of $623 million both come in softer than guided for, concerning amidst a flattish net debt load of $3.20 billion, after more shares were bought back and dividends were paid in the final quarter.

The outlook is anything but convincing, with fiscal 2026 organic net sales seen as between flat and up slightly. While earnings are seen as flattish between $3.30 and $3.60 per share, EBITDA is set to fall to $580-$610 million, which is concerning. At the midpoint of this guidance, leverage ratios will increase to 5.4 times, assuming a flat debt position.

Trust Evaporates

With 70 million shares trading at $17, the market value of the firm stands at $1.2 billion, this excluding the huge debt load. Given a $4.6 billion enterprise valuation, EBITDA multiples look very reasonable at 7-8 times for a relatively stable performing business, with shares trading at just 5 times highly adjusted earnings. This comes amidst the distorted financing structure, i.e., the heavy debt load employed. It is hard to make true comps, as the company does not really have pure-play and publicly traded peers here.

Given all this, it is easily understood that the market lost faith in the adjusted earnings metrics, also as cash flow generation was very soft this year, trailing adjusted earnings by a wide margin. All this makes me hard-pressed to build conviction, as the market is cautious as well, with a near 7% dividend yield not being sustainable.

Even as debt is largely fixed, it comes with relatively compelling interest rates and no meaningful maturities due before 2028. I am cautious. The continued willingness to pay dividends, even buy back stock, and pursue M&A. With leverage being too high, far too high in my view, I am cautious given the market reaction.

Given all this, I am very cautious, and while shares look compelling here, it is hard to build conviction. By now the equity valuation is very modest, as equity essentially has become a call option on the business, which leaves all options for equity holders on the table but makes it hard to build conviction at the same time.

data.title: WhiteFiber: Volatile Times
data.description:
When WhiteFiber (WYFI) went public early in August, I concluded that the AI craze was in full swing. Shares fell below the offer price as the market was skeptical of what essentially is a commodity supplier of AI data center infrastructure.

Valuations appear stretched in relation to tangible assets, with growth depending on debt financing and continued AI demand, making me cautious. Following a boom-bust move in the months following, shares are back to the IPO price as the first quarterly earnings releases do not impress me and do not give me a convincing reason to get involved.

The Business, The IPO

WhiteFiber describes its own business as that of an AI infrastructure solution provider, as the company operates high-performance computing data centers (HPU) and cloud-based HPC graphic compression units (GPU), which allow customers to run AI and machine learning applications.

The background of the business was rather complex, as the company used to be a subsidiary of a company called Bit Digital (BTBT). The company, or better said its parent company, Bit Digital, acquired Enovum about a year ago, a Montreal-based HPC data center with 4 MW AI capacity.

Thereafter, Bit Digital acquired another 5 MW AI center in Montreal and another 7 MW AI center in Quebec, all assets now part of WhiteFiber. The real upcoming growth engine was the purchase of an industrial building in North Carolina in May, set to boost this pipeline by 99 MW, as the company touted a pipeline of another 800 MW of new projects under LOIs and other forms of contract, with the business having 4,500 NVIDIA GPUs deployed.

The company went public at $17 per share, as 35 million shares were awarded a near $600 million equity valuation at that price, this valuation including a $125 million net cash position. This resulted in an operating asset valuation near the half-a-billion mark, marking a big premium to the valuation of these tangible assets on the balance sheet.

Moreover, since operations only started in 2024, the financial numbers were hard to read into. The company reported first-quarter sales at nearly $17 million, with EBITDA reported just shy of $6 million. The company guided for second-quarter sales at $18 million and change.

With these essentially rental revenues trending around $75 million per annum and positive EBITDA numbers reported, I felt cautious given the valuation. Moreover, this certainly was the case, as this essentially was a lease model, including that of assets that depreciate rapidly, read the GPUs.

With shares ending the first day of trading at $16 and change, I erred on the cautious side, deciding not to get involved here.

A Mini Boom Bust

Since the offering, shares of WhiteFiber have risen to levels around the $40 mark by the middle of October. What followed was a big pullback to current levels at $17 and change, at par compared to the initial offer price.

In September, WhiteFiber reported definitive second quarter results, with second quarter sales up 48% to $18.7 million. Adjusted EBITDA only came in at $3.3 million, marking a real deleverage on that front, with GAAP operating losses reported in excess of $9 million. The company confirmed that Bit Digital owned 71% of the shares as the company closed the NC-1 acquisition, a one million square foot data center in North Carolina, with deployment starting for the MTL-3 development as well.

Early in November, third-quarter sales were reported up 65% to $20.2 million, yet the added costs of being a listed business made that adjusted EBITDA fall to $2.3 million, while GAAP operating losses rose to more than $14 million. Further growth is seen as the company completed the deployment of the MTL-3 development as of October, now being a revenue-generating facility. This brings year-end MW online capacity to 16 MW, a number seen increasing by a factor of nearly 5 times to 76 MW by year-end 2026.

By now cash holdings have come down to $166 million as the company is very liquid following the IPO, but this will deplete rapidly with capital spending totaling $145 million in the first nine months of the year, with depreciation charges being just a fraction of that number.

And Now?

The truth is that while shares are back to square one after a big ride higher and subsequent move lower, I am very cautious here on shares of WhiteFiber. I am still put off by an opaque shareholder structure in which its former parent still holds a majority stake in the business, and the parent actually trades at a discount compared to the proportionate stake in WhiteFiber. Also, there are heavy losses (certainly if we factor in the costs from being a publicly listed business), heavy capital expenditure requirements, and an uncertain outlook here.

While the AI craze continues, although witnessing continued waves of doubts, I am erring cautious at the same time as well. Given all these trends and the situation, I simply cannot do else than to err cautious. Right now, modest sequential revenue growth comes with continued losses and large investment requirements, given the huge increase in targeted capacity expansion in 2026.

Given all this, it is easy for me to err on the cautious side, as I am by no means enticed to buy this dip here. With the situation being far too opaque, losses reported, and capital intensity being high, I cannot build conviction here.

WhiteFiber remains an interesting proxy for the AI craze here and an interesting story to watch unfold given the aggressive growth seen next year, but I see no compelling reasons to get involved here.

data.title: Thornburg Small/Mid Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary

Market Review

Markets delivered solid gains in the third quarter, extending the recovery that began in late spring, buoyed by renewed optimism over easing monetary policy, solid corporate earnings, and resilient investor sentiment. The Federal Reserve delivered its long-anticipated rate cut in September, lowering rates by 25 basis points in response to signs of a cooling labor market and broader economic moderation. While uncertainty surrounding the U.S. election cycle and the Trump administration’s ongoing trade negotiations remained an overhang, the broad economic backdrop proved supportive for risk assets.

Small and mid-cap equities outperformed large caps during the quarter, benefiting from expectations of lower rates and increased investor appetite for domestic cyclicality. At the sector level, information technology delivered another strong quarter, with semiconductor and software companies supported by increased spending on data centers and power infrastructure tied to AI adoption. Industrials also outperformed as companies with exposure to transportation, aerospace supply chains, and specialized manufacturing reported improving order trends. The consumer staples sector, with a bias towards companies with more defensive characteristics, lagged during the risk on rally.

data.title: Voya Infrastructure, Industrials And Materials Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Fund seeks potential enhanced stability of returns over a market cycle through investments in global companies with potential to benefit from increased infrastructure spending, and by selling call options on select equity Indexes or exchange-traded funds.

Key takeaways

During the quarter, the Fund benefitted from the performance of the core model, particularly the sentiment and valuation pillars. The ESG pillar within the core model detracted the most followed by the smaller market capitalization positioning.

Inflation is easing, labor markets are softening, and the U.S. Federal Reserve has pivoted to rate cuts. Despite tariff pressures, consumer sensitivity and constrained spending should limit inflation persistence. U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth is projected to remain positive but moderate, with large-cap equities favored due to strong fundamental factors and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven growth potential.

Global momentum is mixed. Europe and the United Kingdom face fiscal and structural headwinds, while Japan shows promise amid reforms. China struggles with property sector stress and cautious consumers, though offshore equities benefit from technology exposure. International bonds offer less attractive yields, and emerging markets (EM) debt faces uneven fundamental factors.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Fund provided a total return of 7.11% on a net asset value basis, and a total return of 6.35% on a market price basis. For the same period, the Fund's reference index, the Voya Infrastructure Industrials and Materials Custom Index (the Index), returned 7.70%.

Equity Portfolio

For the reporting period, the equity sleeve of the Fund modestly outperformed the Index, due to positive performance from the core model, particularly the sentiment and valuation pillars. The ESG pillar within the core model detracted the most followed by the smaller market capitalization.

On the regional level portfolio holdings in Asia excluding-Japan and Canada regions contributed while portfolio holdings in Japan and the United States detracted.

At the sector level stock selection in industrials and utilities contributed. Among the key individual contributors were the overweight positions in Delta Electronics, Inc., Barrick Mining Corp. (B), and Central Japan Railway Co (OTCPK:CJPRY).

Conversely, stock selection within information technology and communication services detracted. At the stock level, an underweight position in SoftBank Group Corp., not owning Corning Inc., and an underweight position in Amphenol Corp. detracted the most.

Option Portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a negative impact on returns. The covered call strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implemented this strategy by typically writing call options on sector and regional exchange-traded funds (ETFs), the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to closely track the Fund's reference Index. The stirke prices of the options written were typically at or near the money, with expiration dates around one month at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

Emerging markets outperformed, led by North Asia's technology strength. Taiwan and Korea advanced thanks to the semiconductor boom, offsetting weakness in China and slower growth in Latin America and Africa. Investors became more selective later in the quarter, favoring countries with strong finances and clear policies.

Developed markets saw broad, steady growth. European industrials, banks, and energy companies benefited from improving economic data, while defensive sectors in Switzerland and the United Kingdom helped limit volatility. The weaker U.S. dollar boosted returns for U.S. investors, and the U.S Federal Reserve's September interest rate cut lifted market confidence. Value stocks gained momentum in banks and energy, while quality stocks stayed resilient as inflation eased.

Key takeaways

Fund performance

Invesco Intermediate Term Municipal Income Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) underperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal Bond 2- 17 Year Investment Grade Index.

Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Investment objective

The fund seeks to provide investors with a high level of current income exempt from federal income tax, consistent with preservation of capital.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 2,106.57

Portfolio managers

Elizabeth Mossow, Jack Connelly, John Schorle, Joshua Cooney, Julius Williams, Mark Paris, Rebecca Setcavage, Tim O'Reilly

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the third quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 2.87%, 1.58% and 2.66%, respectively.1 The municipal market saw mixed performance: July and August continued the previous quarter's underperformance, but September brought strong performance in long duration municipals as US Treasuries rallied and municipal bond fund flows increased.
  • New municipal bond issuance reached $153 billion in the third quarter, bringing the year-to- date total to $437 billion. This is 13% higher than the same period in 2024 and significantly above previous years.1
  • Municipal fund net flows were a highlight, totaling about $16.2 billion for the quarter and $31.8 billion year to date.2
  • In September, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) made its first rate cut of the year, lowering the federal funds rate by 0.25%. The Fed cited increased downside risks to employment and reiterated its commitment to balancing its dual mandate of maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. Future policy decisions will depend on incoming data, the evolving outlook and the balance of risks.3
  • We believe state and municipal budgets remain healthy. Although the pace of credit rating upgrades has slowed, credit fundamentals still appear strong and upgrades have outpaced downgrades so far this year.4
  • The quarter ended with the threat of a government shutdown as Congress struggled to pass funding bills needed to keep federal agencies operating in the fiscal year beginning October 1st.1
  • During periods of policy uncertainty, we rely on our experienced credit research team to identify and capitalize on marketplace dislocations in order to deliver value to shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see opportunities in the municipal bond market. With potential for further Fed rate cuts and steady new municipal issuance, we believe high absolute yields and strong fundamentals make municipals an appealing option.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for duration and are looking to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate and according to prospectus guidelines. We maintain an overweight in the short and intermediate maturity segments of the yield curve given the fund’s mandate.

Sector Allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR), airport and dedicated tax sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market as upgrades of credit ratings have continued to outpace downgrades. Various sectors have been under pressure due to higher labor costs, along with uncertainty about federal funding and tariffs. We are monitoring these sectors closely and continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to find pockets of opportunity.

Performance highlights

The third quarter had a volatile start as record levels of new issuance and uncertainty related to fiscal and monetary policies weighed on municipal valuations. Momentum shifted in late August as labor market weakness fueled expectations that the Fed would resume monetary easing. Municipals rallied strongly through September and ended the quarter with positive returns. As expected, the Fed cut the federal funds rate by 0.25% in September and its comments on future actions indicated a stance favoring easier monetary policy. Yields fell across the municipal yield curve, with yields on 10- and 30-year bonds down 0.28% and 0.23%, respectively.5 Higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed for the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

Security selection among dedicated tax revenue bonds and airport bonds added to relative performance during the quarter. Both underweights and security selection in bonds rated AAA and AA added to relative performance. From a duration perspective, an underweight in bonds with durations between two and six years contributed to relative performance.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

Security selection among IDR/PCR bonds detracted from relative return during the quarter. From a duration perspective, the fund's overweight in bonds with durations less than one year detracted from relative return.

About Risk

All or a portion of the Fund’s otherwise tax-exempt income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax.

An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

Inverse floating rate obligations may be subject to greater price volatility than a fixed income security with similar qualities. When short-term interest rates rise, they may decrease in value and produce less or no income and are subject to risks similar to derivatives.

Junk bonds have greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. Junk bond values fluctuate more than high quality bonds and can decline significantly over a short time.

The Fund may hold illiquid securities that it may be unable to sell at the preferred time or price and could lose its entire investment in such securities.

Securities which are in the medium- and lower-grade categories generally offer higher yields than are offered by higher-grade securities of similar maturity, but they also generally involve more volatility and greater risks, such as greater credit risk, market risk, liquidity risk, management risk, and regulatory risk.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

The Fund may invest in municipal securities issued by entities having similar characteristics, which may make the fund more susceptible to fluctuation.

Municipal securities are subject to the risk that legislative or economic conditions could affect an issuer’s ability to make principal and/or interest payments.

There is no guarantee that the Fund's income will be exempt from federal and state income taxes.

Based on a Master Settlement Agreement (“MSA”) with 46 states and six other US jurisdictions, large US tobacco manufacturers have agreed to make annual payments to government entities in exchange for the release of all litigation claims. Several states have sold bonds backed by those future payments, including (i) bonds that make payments only from a state’s interest in the MSA and (ii) bonds that make payments from both the MSA revenue and from an “appropriation pledge” by the state which requires the state to pass a specific periodic appropriation to make the payments and is generally not an unconditional guarantee of payment by a state. Settlement payments are based on factors, including, but not limited to, annual domestic cigarette shipments, cigarette consumption, inflation and the financial capability of participating tobacco companies. Payments could be reduced if consumption decreases, if market share is lost to non- MSA manufacturers, or if there is a negative outcome in litigation regarding the MSA, including challenges by participating tobacco manufacturers regarding the amount of annual payments owed under the MSA.

Ratings source: Standard & Poor's, Moody's or Fitch, as applicable. A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments or other debts. Ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest); ratings are subject to change without notice. Not Rated (NR) indicates the debtor was not rated and should not be interpreted as indicating low quality. A negative in Cash indicates fund activity that has accrued or is pending settlement. Pre-Refunded/Escrowed to Maturity (Prerefunded/ETM) bonds are issues backed by an escrow account, invested in US Treasuries, which is used to pay bondholders. Other includes bonds rated below single B and bonds currently not paying a coupon. For more information on rating methodologies, please visit the following NRSRO websites: www.standardandpoors.com and select 'Understanding Credit Ratings' under Rating Resources 'About Ratings' on the homepage; What Is a Credit Rating? | Understanding Credit Ratings - Moody's and select 'Understanding Ratings' on the homepage; www.fitchratings.com and select 'Ratings Definitions Criteria' under 'Resources' on the homepage. * 30-day SEC yield is based on a 30-day period and is computed by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. 30-day SEC unsubsidized yield reflects the 30-day yield if the investment adviser were not waiving all or part of its fee or reimbursing the fund for part of its expenses. Total return would have also been lower in the absence of these temporary reimbursements or waivers. Option adjusted duration is a measure, as estimated by the fund's portfolio managers, of a bond fund's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. It takes into account mortgage prepayments, puts, adjustable coupons and potential call dates. Tax equivalent 30-day SEC yield quoted above is based on the 2025 top federal tax rate of 40.8%, including the 3.8% tax on earned income under the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act, as applicable. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed, the yield would have been lower. Average effective maturity is a weighted average of all the maturities of the bonds in a portfolio, computed by weighting each bond's effective maturity by the market value of the security. It takes into account mortgage prepayments, puts, adjustable coupons and potential call dates.
